required.title: A style guide
required.author: Strunk and White (for editing reference). It is short, clear, and opinionated. The student should own a copy and consult it during editing.

competence_markers.description: The student can take a writing task through all four stages — prewriting, drafting, revision, editing — as a repeatable process
The student uses at least two prewriting methods and can explain which ones work best for different tasks
The student writes first drafts with forward momentum, resisting the urge to self-edit during drafting
The student revises at all three levels — structural, paragraph, and sentence — and can demonstrate each in their revision portfolio
The student participates in peer review, giving feedback that is specific, honest, and constructive
The student edits as a separate final pass, applying grammar and mechanics knowledge from 3.1.2
The student has assembled at least three revision portfolios showing the complete process from prewriting to final version
The student writes three to five pages per week across multiple forms and maintains a daily writing practice

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through the writing process.
narration_prompts.detail: What are the four stages? What is the job of each stage? Why do you do them in order?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me a piece from your Revision Portfolio.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you discover during prewriting that was not in your original plan? What changed between the first draft and the final version? What was the hardest revision decision you made?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between revision and editing?
Describe your experience giving peer feedback.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice about the other writer's work? How did you decide what to say? What did you learn about your own writing from reading someone else's?

narration_prompts.prompt: What prewriting method works best for you?
narration_prompts.detail: Why? Is it different for different kinds of writing?
Have you ever cut something from your writing that you loved? What happened to the piece when you did?

cross_connections.description: The writing process developed here is directly applied to essay writing. Prewriting becomes thesis development and outlining. Revision becomes the discipline of testing every paragraph against the argument. The process is the engine; the essay is the vehicle.

Creative writing follows the same process but with different emphases. Prewriting for fiction may be character sketches or scene planning. Revision for fiction focuses on narrative arc, character consistency, and voice. The process adapts to the form.

The research paper is the most process-intensive writing form. Every stage of the writing process — from research-based prewriting to evidence-integrated drafting to source-verified revision — applies directly.

The writing process is craftsmanship applied to language. The discipline of revising through multiple passes, the willingness to cut what does not serve the piece, the standard of "good enough to defend" — these are IC2 principles in action.

The discipline of outlining and revision translates directly to speech preparation. A well-organized essay and a well-organized speech follow the same structural principles. The student who masters the writing process can prepare a speech with the same rigor.

Historical writing — analytical essays, source-based arguments, narrative accounts — is a primary application of the writing process at Weathering Storms. The student who can take a historical question through prewriting, drafting, and revision is doing genuine intellectual work.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been writing for years now. You keep a notebook. You can freewrite without stopping. You have revised your own work and seen it get better. What you have been doing intuitively, you are now going to learn to do systematically.

The writing process is not a suggestion. It is the method by which almost all good writing gets made. It has stages — prewriting, drafting, revision, editing — and each stage does a different job. The writer who tries to do all the stages simultaneously produces mediocre work, because the creative mind and the critical mind interfere with each other when they operate at the same time. The writer who separates the stages — who generates freely in the drafting phase and critiques rigorously in the revision phase — produces work that is both more creative and more polished.

This packet makes the process explicit. You will learn prewriting methods that help you think before you write. You will learn to draft without self-censoring. You will learn to revise at multiple levels — the whole piece, the paragraph, the sentence. You will learn that editing is the final pass, not the first. And you will build a revision portfolio that documents your growth as a writer.

This is the difference between a person who writes and a person who has a writing practice. The first depends on inspiration. The second depends on discipline. Inspiration is unreliable. Discipline is not.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Prewriting as a thinking discipline** — multiple methods for organizing thought before drafting
2. **Outlining as structural planning** — building a blueprint for a piece of writing
3. **Drafting as exploration** — writing a first draft without self-editing, with the understanding that revision follows
4. **Multi-level revision** — revising at the structural level, the paragraph level, and the sentence level
5. **Peer review skills** — the ability to read another writer's work and provide specific, useful feedback
6. **Editing as a separate final pass** — grammar, punctuation, and word choice addressed after structural revision is complete
7. **The revision portfolio** — documentation of the writing process from first draft to finished piece
8. **Writing volume and discipline** — three to five pages of new writing per week across all forms
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Four Stages of the Writing Process

Every piece of writing you produce from now on will pass through four stages. You will not always do them in strict order — sometimes you will loop back from revision to drafting, or from editing back to revision. But you will always do all four, and you will learn to recognize which stage you are in and what that stage requires.

**Stage 1: Prewriting** — Thinking before writing. This is where you figure out what you want to say, who you are saying it to, and how you want to organize it. Prewriting methods include freewriting on the topic, outlining, mind-mapping, listing questions you want the piece to answer, and conversation. The student who skips prewriting and starts drafting immediately is like a builder who starts nailing boards without a blueprint. Sometimes it works. Usually, it produces a mess that requires tearing down and starting over.

**Stage 2: Drafting** — Writing the first version. The first draft is an act of discovery — you find out what you actually think by trying to say it. The critical rule of drafting: do not self-edit. Do not stop to fix the sentence you just wrote. Do not go back and revise the opening paragraph. Write forward. Get the whole piece down. You can fix it later. Right now, you are building the raw material that revision will shape.

**Stage 3: Revision** — Seeing your writing again and making it better. Revision operates at three levels, and you work from the largest to the smallest:
- *Structural revision:* Does the piece do what I intended? Is the argument in the right order? Are there sections that should be cut, moved, or expanded? Does the piece hold together as a whole?
- *Paragraph revision:* Does each paragraph do one job? Does each paragraph connect to the one before it and the one after it? Are there paragraphs that wander or that try to do too many things?
- *Sentence revision:* Is each sentence as clear as I can make it? Are there wasted words? Does the rhythm of the sentences serve the meaning?

**Stage 4: Editing** — The final mechanical pass. This is where you fix grammar, punctuation, spelling, and word choice. Editing happens last because there is no point in polishing a sentence that you might cut during revision. Edit only after you are satisfied with the structure, the paragraphs, and the sentences.

### Part 2: Prewriting Methods

Different writing tasks need different prewriting approaches. Learn them all, then use the ones that work best for each situation.

**Freewriting on the topic:** You already know how to freewrite. Now use it as a prewriting tool: write for ten minutes about your topic, without stopping, without organizing, without censoring. When the timer goes off, read what you wrote and highlight the strongest ideas. These become the seeds of your draft.

**Outlining:** An outline is a structural plan. It answers the question: "In what order will I present my ideas?" A formal outline uses Roman numerals and letters. An informal outline is just a numbered list of points. The key is that the outline is written before the draft, and the draft follows the outline — though you are free to deviate from the outline when the writing takes you somewhere better.

How to test your outline: look at each point and ask, "If I remove this, does the piece still hold together?" If yes, the point is not essential and might be cut. If no, it is structural and must stay. Then look at the order: does each point build on the one before it? Could the reader follow this sequence?

**Mind-mapping:** Start with your central topic in the middle of a blank page. Draw branches outward for each main idea. Draw sub-branches for supporting points, examples, and details. The visual format helps you see relationships between ideas that a linear list might miss.

**Listing questions:** Write down every question your piece should answer. Then arrange the questions in the order a reader would need them answered. The questions become the framework of your draft.

**Talking it out:** Explain your topic to someone — a mentor, a peer, a sibling. The act of verbal explanation forces you to organize your thoughts. After the conversation, write an outline based on what you said. Speaking is often faster than writing for figuring out what you think.

### Part 3: Drafting as Exploration

The first draft is not the product. It is the process of finding out what you think.

This is hard to believe until you experience it. You sit down to write an essay about why rivers mattered to early civilizations, and you think you know what you want to say. Then, three paragraphs in, you discover an idea you did not anticipate — a connection between irrigation and political authority that was not in your outline. That discovery is the draft doing its job. The first draft is how your mind explores the territory of the subject. It is supposed to be messy. It is supposed to surprise you.

**The discipline of drafting:** Write forward. Do not revise as you go. If you write a bad sentence, leave it and keep going. If you realize your second paragraph should actually be your first paragraph, make a note and keep going. If you discover that your thesis is wrong, write the new thesis and keep going. You will fix everything in revision. Right now, your only job is to get the whole piece on paper.

The internal critic — the voice that says "that sentence is terrible" — must be silenced during drafting. It gets its turn during revision. During drafting, it is worse than useless — it interrupts the generative process and produces timid, overworked prose. Write badly on purpose if you have to. You can make it good later. You cannot make it good if it does not exist.

### Part 4: Revision at Three Levels

Revision is where writing actually happens. The difference between a mediocre writer and a good one is almost never talent. It is revision. The good writer is the one who looks at their first draft, recognizes its problems, and has the discipline and skill to fix them.

**Level 1 — Structural Revision (the whole piece):**
Read your draft from beginning to end. Then ask:
- Does this piece say what I intended it to say?
- Is the opening strong enough that a reader would keep reading?
- Is the conclusion satisfying, or does it just stop?
- Is the piece in the right order? Would moving sections improve the flow?
- Are there sections that are not necessary? (Cut them, even if you like them.)
- Are there gaps — places where the reader needs more information or explanation?

**Level 2 — Paragraph Revision:**
Examine each paragraph individually. Ask:
- What is this paragraph's one job? Can I state it in a single sentence?
- Does every sentence in this paragraph serve that job?
- Does this paragraph connect clearly to the one before it? (Transitions)
- Does this paragraph lead naturally to the one after it?

**Level 3 — Sentence Revision:**
Read each sentence. Ask:
- Is this sentence as clear as I can make it?
- Are there words that can be cut without losing meaning?
- Is the sentence too long? (If you run out of breath reading it aloud, it is too long.)
- Is the word choice precise? ("She walked" is vague. "She shuffled," "she strode," "she limped" — each tells a different story.)

### Part 5: Peer Review — Learning to Help Another Writer

Giving good feedback on someone else's writing is one of the most powerful ways to improve your own. When you read another writer's work and identify its strengths and problems, you develop the same analytical skills that you apply to your own revision.

**Rules for useful feedback:**
- "I liked it" is not useful. Everyone likes hearing it, but it tells the writer nothing about their craft.
- "I got confused in the second paragraph because I couldn't tell whether you were talking about Rome or Greece" is useful. It identifies a specific place and a specific problem.
- "I think you should change this to ____" is less useful than "This part did not work for me, and here is why." The writer needs to understand the problem so they can solve it themselves.
- Always identify at least one thing that works well. The writer needs to know what to keep, not just what to fix.
- Read the piece twice before giving feedback. The first read is for overall impression. The second is for specific observations.

### Part 6: Editing — The Final Pass

Editing is separate from revision. Revision changes what you say and how you say it. Editing fixes the mechanics: grammar, punctuation, spelling, and word choice.

Common editing targets:
- Subject-verb agreement
- Consistent verb tense
- Comma usage (especially comma splices — two complete sentences joined by a comma instead of a period or semicolon)
- Apostrophes (it's versus its; their/they're/there)
- Spelling errors that spellcheck misses (form/from, then/than, affect/effect)
- Repeated words (the same word used three times in two sentences)
- Wordiness (cutting unnecessary words: "due to the fact that" becomes "because"; "at this point in time" becomes "now")

Keep a copy of *The Elements of Style* nearby during editing. When you are unsure about a rule, look it up. Over time, the rules become automatic.

### Part 7: The Revision Portfolio

Three times per year (or more), you will take a piece of writing through the complete process — prewriting, drafting, revision, editing — and document the journey. Your Revision Portfolio contains:

1. The prewriting (outline, freewrite, mind map — whatever you used)
2. The first draft
3. Notes from revision (what you identified as problems, what you decided to change)
4. The revised draft(s) — there may be more than one revision pass
5. The final, edited version

This portfolio is the primary evidence of your growth as a writer. It shows not just what you wrote, but how you wrote it — and that you understand the process well enough to explain your decisions.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Writing Practice [Ongoing]
The student writes three to five pages per week across all forms — journal, essay, creative work, correspondence. The daily notebook continues. Freewriting continues. This is not a new assignment; it is the continuation and expansion of the habit established in Stretching Branches.

### Study 2: Prewriting Exploration [Early in the Packet]
The student practices each prewriting method on the same topic: freewriting, outlining, mind-mapping, listing questions, talking it out. Then they reflect: which method helped them most? Which felt most natural? Which revealed ideas the others did not? The goal is not to find one method but to develop a repertoire.

### Study 3: The Drafting Discipline [Ongoing]
For each major writing assignment, the student writes a complete first draft in a single session (or two sessions for longer pieces). The draft is written quickly, without self-editing. The student practices the discipline of forward motion — writing through difficulty rather than stopping to fix.

### Study 4: Multi-Level Revision Workshop [At Least 4 Times During the Packet]
The student takes a first draft through all three levels of revision (structural, paragraph, sentence) with guidance from the mentor. Each level is addressed in a separate pass. The mentor asks questions; the student makes decisions. Over the course of the packet, the student takes increasing ownership of the revision process.

### Study 5: Peer Review Practice [At Least 3 Times During the Packet]
The student exchanges drafts with a peer (or the mentor acting as peer) and provides written feedback following the peer review guidelines. The feedback is specific, honest, and constructive. After receiving feedback, the student decides which suggestions to adopt and which to set aside — and can explain their reasoning.

### Study 6: Revision Portfolio Assembly [3 Times Per Year]
The student selects a piece of writing, takes it through the complete process, and assembles the portfolio documenting each stage. The mentor reviews the portfolio not for the quality of the final piece alone but for the quality of the process: Is there evidence of genuine prewriting? Does the first draft show forward momentum? Does the revision address structural issues, not just mechanics?

### Study 7: "Kill Your Darlings" Exercise [Once During the Packet]
The student identifies their favorite sentence or paragraph in a recent piece of writing — and cuts it. Not because it is bad, but because the piece is better without it. This exercise teaches the hardest lesson in revision: that good writing sometimes requires sacrificing things you love because they do not serve the whole. After cutting, the student reads the piece with and without the darling, and assesses which version is stronger.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Process Fluency
**Developing well:** The student moves through prewriting, drafting, revision, and editing as distinct stages without prompting. They can name which stage they are in and what it requires. They do not try to draft and revise simultaneously.

**Developing slowly:** The student still treats writing as a single-pass activity. They either over-plan (endless prewriting, never drafting) or under-plan (jump straight to drafting without any prewriting). The stages feel like arbitrary hoops rather than a useful structure.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you modeled the process yourself? Has the student seen the four stages produce a result that is clearly better than a single-pass effort? Sometimes the most powerful demonstration is writing a piece through the process alongside the student and comparing the result to a quick, unprocessed draft.

### 2. Revision Depth
**Developing well:** The student's revisions show structural changes: reorganized sections, cut paragraphs, expanded arguments, strengthened openings and conclusions. The revised version is significantly different from the first draft in ways that go beyond corrected mechanics.

**Developing slowly:** Revisions are cosmetic: fixed spelling, added a word here and there, maybe combined two sentences. The structure, argument, and content are unchanged from the first draft.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you guiding revision through questions at all three levels? Is the student revising immediately after drafting (too close to the work) or after some time has passed (fresh eyes)? A day between drafting and revision makes an enormous difference.

### 3. Peer Review Quality
**Developing well:** The student's feedback is specific, identifies both strengths and problems, and focuses on the piece rather than the person. The student can receive feedback without defensiveness and can explain which suggestions they adopted and why.

**Developing slowly:** Feedback is vague ("it's good") or entirely negative ("this doesn't make sense"). The student either accepts all feedback without judgment or rejects all feedback defensively.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you taught and modeled the peer review protocol explicitly? Have you practiced giving feedback on a sample piece together before asking the student to do it independently?

### 4. Writing Volume and Habit
**Developing well:** The student writes three to five pages per week without complaint. Daily writing is habitual. The student sometimes writes voluntarily outside of assigned work. Writing feels like a normal part of life, not a special event.

**Developing slowly:** Writing happens only when assigned. The student produces the minimum and stops. There is no voluntary writing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Does the student have genuine choice in topics and forms? Is writing always an assignment, or is it sometimes a conversation, a letter, a journal entry? Volume comes from engagement, and engagement comes from ownership.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Process as Busywork
If the student experiences the writing process as four hoops to jump through — "I have to do prewriting because the packet says so" — the process has been taught as a compliance exercise rather than a thinking tool. The student needs to experience the process producing a result they could not have achieved without it. Choose a topic that is genuinely complex. Have the student draft it without prewriting. Then have them take a different topic through the full process. Compare the results. When the student sees the difference, the process sells itself.

### Premature Editing
Students gravitate toward editing because it feels productive and because it is easier than structural revision. Fixing a comma is satisfying. Realizing that your third paragraph should be your first paragraph is uncomfortable. Watch for students who "revise" by editing and redirect them to structural questions.

### Neglecting the Daily Practice
The formal process work (prewriting, drafting, revision, editing) can feel like the "real" work, and the daily notebook can fall away. Do not let this happen. The notebook and freewriting practice are the fluency engine that powers everything else. A student who writes only process-driven pieces loses the generative freedom that produces their best material.

### Toxic Peer Review
Peer review can go wrong in two directions: too nice (every piece is "great!") or too harsh (brutal criticism that damages confidence). Teach the protocol explicitly. Model it. Supervise early sessions. The student needs to understand that useful feedback is specific and respectful — it serves the work, not the reviewer's ego.
